title: AI Skills Architecture — Composable Capabilities at Scale
description: AI Skills are composable, reusable capability modules that bridge the gap between stateless tools and autonomous agents. At FAANG Staff+ level, you design skill frameworks that enable cross-team sharing, dynamic composition, hierarchical planning, and production-grade reliability across hundreds of skills.

sections.title: What Are AI Skills? — Skills vs Tools vs Agents
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The AI capability stack has four layers. Tools are stateless functions (search, calculate, read file) — they do one thing, take inputs, return outputs. Skills are stateful, composable capability modules that may chain multiple tools, maintain context, and implement logic (e.g., a "customer support" skill that searches the KB, checks order status, and drafts a reply). Agents are autonomous loops that plan, execute, and adapt — they select and orchestrate skills dynamically. Frameworks (Semantic Kernel, LangChain) provide the runtime for skills and agents. The key Staff-level insight: skills are the unit of reuse, sharing, and governance. An organization with 100+ AI capabilities should model them as skills, not as flat tool lists.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Microsoft's Semantic Kernel (the most mature skill framework) defines skills as the primary abstraction. A typical enterprise AI deployment has: 50-200 skills, each composed of 3-10 functions. Skill reuse rate: 40-60% across teams (compared to <10% for raw tools). Skill development time: 1-3 days per skill (vs 2-4 weeks per custom agent). Skill chaining depth: typically 2-5 skills per agent task. Skills reduce prompt engineering effort by 60% (via shared, tested prompt templates within skills).

track_changes Decision Framework: Use a tool when: the capability is a single stateless operation with no internal logic or state. Use a skill when: the capability requires multiple steps, maintains state, or encapsulates business logic. Use an agent when: the task requires dynamic planning, adaptation, and multi-turn execution. Tools are building blocks for skills; skills are building blocks for agents. At FAANG, most production AI systems use all three: agents at the top, skills in the middle, tools at the bottom.
sections.codeBlock:
# The AI Capability Hierarchy
from typing import Any, Callable
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class CapabilityLevel(Enum):
    TOOL = "tool"     # Stateless, single operation
    SKILL = "skill"   # Stateful, composed, reusable
    AGENT = "agent"   # Autonomous, planning, adapting


@dataclass
class CapabilityInterface:
    """Unified interface for tools, skills, and agents."""
    name: str
    description: str
    level: CapabilityLevel
    input_schema: dict
    output_schema: dict


# === A Tool (stateless) ===
class SearchTool:
    level = CapabilityLevel.TOOL

    async def execute(self, query: str, max_results: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
        """Single operation: search returns results."""
        return self._search(query)[:max_results]

    def _search(self, query):
        return [{"title": "result", "url": "..."}]


# === A Skill (stateful, composed) ===
class CustomerSupportSkill:
    """
    A skill that composes multiple tools and maintains session context.
    Skills are the unit of business capability.
    """
    level = CapabilityLevel.SKILL

    def __init__(self, search_tool: SearchTool, ticket_system: Any, llm: Callable):
        self.search = search_tool
        self.tickets = ticket_system
        self.llm = llm
        self.session_context: dict = {}

    async def handle_inquiry(self, user_id: str, message: str) -> str:
        """
        Composes multiple steps:
        1. Check if user has open tickets
        2. Search KB for relevant articles
        3. Draft response using LLM
        """
        # Step 1: Get context
        open_tickets = await self.tickets.get_open(user_id)
        self.session_context["open_tickets"] = open_tickets

        # Step 2: Search knowledge base
        kb_results = await self.search.execute(message, max_results=3)

        # Step 3: LLM drafts response using context
        prompt = f"""User inquiry: {message}
Open tickets: {open_tickets}
KB articles: {kb_results}

Draft a helpful response."""
        response = await self.llm(prompt)

        self.session_context["last_response"] = response
        return response


# === An Agent (autonomous) ===
class SupportAgent:
    """
    An agent that dynamically selects and orchestrates skills.
    """
    level = CapabilityLevel.AGENT

    def __init__(self, skills: dict[str, CustomerSupportSkill]):
        self.skills = skills
        self.plan: list[str] = []

    async def run(self, task: str) -> str:
        # Plan which skills to use
        skills_to_use = self._plan(task)
        self.plan = skills_to_use

        # Execute skills in order
        results = []
        for skill_name in skills_to_use:
            skill = self.skills[skill_name]
            result = await skill.handle_inquiry("user_42", task)
            results.append(result)

        return "\n".join(results)

    def _plan(self, task: str) -> list[str]:
        """Dynamic skill selection based on task."""
        if "billing" in task.lower():
            return ["billing_skill", "escalation_skill"]
        if "technical" in task.lower():
            return ["troubleshooting_skill"]
        return ["general_support_skill"]


# Staff insight: The hierarchy is not rigid.
# Some skills may act as agents (if they plan internally).
# Some tools may be skills (if they encapsulate logic).
# The key is the interface: all three levels expose
# name + description + execute(params) -> result.

sections.title: Skill Definition & Composition Patterns
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: A skill has four parts: a name and description (for discovery by agents), an input/output contract (JSON Schema), an implementation (can call tools, other skills, or LLMs), and state (context from previous invocations). Skills compose via chaining (output of skill A → input of skill B), routing (a router skill dispatches to sub-skills based on input), parallel fan-out (multiple skills execute concurrently and results merge), and fallback chains (try skill A, if fails try skill B). The key design principle: skills should be single-responsibility (do one thing well), discoverable (rich descriptions for agent selection), and observable (emit metrics for monitoring).

bar_chart Concrete numbers: A well-designed skill: 10-50 lines of logic, 3-10 internal function calls, 1-3 external tool invocations, 0-2 LLM calls. Skill development time: 1-3 days (vs 2-4 weeks for an agent). Skill maintenance cost: ~20% of the original build cost per year (for updates and adapting to LLM changes). Skill chaining: 80% of tasks are completed with 1-2 skills, 15% need 3-5 skills, 5% need 5+ skills. Performance: each chained skill adds 500ms-2s of latency (tool calls + LLM calls).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Identify a business capability (e.g., "handle refund request"). 2) Define the input (order_id, reason) and output (refund_status, message). 3) Implement the skill using internal tools (lookup order, check policy, process refund, notify customer). 4) Add a clear description so agents know when to use this skill. 5) Register the skill in the skill registry. 6) Test the skill independently before composing it into an agent. 7) Monitor skill usage and performance in production.

sections.title: Skill Registries, Discovery & Cross-Team Sharing
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: A skill registry is the catalog where all skills are registered, discoverable, and versioned. A searchable registry with rich metadata enables cross-team reuse — the billing team's "validate_payment" skill is discoverable by the support agent team. The registry stores: skill manifest (name, description, schemas), version history, owner team, usage metrics, and health status. Agents discover skills through the registry at runtime, filtering by capability, domain, and quality metrics.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Microsoft's internal skill registry (based on Semantic Kernel) indexes 10,000+ skills across the company. Average skill discovery latency: <100ms (cached). Cross-team reuse rate: 35% of skills are used by more than one team. Skills with high reuse (5+ teams) have: clear descriptions, stable APIs, comprehensive tests, documented examples. The registry reduces duplicate skill development by 40% (one team builds a skill, others discover and reuse).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Design the registry schema: id, name, description, version, owner, tags, input/output schemas, quality metrics, usage stats. 2) Build a registry API: register, update, deprecate, list, search. 3) Implement semantic search: embed skill descriptions, enable natural language discovery ("find skills that can summarize documents"). 4) Add quality gates: skills must pass tests, have documentation, and meet performance SLAs to be listed. 5) Track usage: which agents call which skills, how often, success rate, latency. 6) Implement deprecation with migration paths.
sections.codeBlock:
# Skill Registry: Discovery, Versioning & Governance
from typing import Any
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
import json

@dataclass
class SkillManifest:
    """Metadata for skill registration."""
    name: str
    description: str
    version: str          # semver
    owner_team: str
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    input_schema: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    output_schema: dict = field(default_factory=dict)
    created_at: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now().isoformat())
    status: str = "active"  # active | deprecated | retired


class SkillRegistry:
    """
    Central registry for AI skills with discovery, versioning, and governance.
    Inspired by Microsoft's Semantic Kernel skill registry.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.skills: dict[str, list[SkillManifest]] = {}  # name -> versions
        self.embeddings: dict[str, list[float]] = {}  # name -> embedding vector

    def register(self, manifest: SkillManifest):
        """Register a new skill version."""
        if manifest.name not in self.skills:
            self.skills[manifest.name] = []
        self.skills[manifest.name].append(manifest)
        # Generate embedding for semantic search
        self._index(manifest)

    def get_latest(self, name: str) -> SkillManifest | None:
        """Get the latest active version of a skill."""
        versions = self.skills.get(name, [])
        active = [v for v in versions if v.status == "active"]
        if not active:
            return None
        return sorted(active, key=lambda v: v.version, reverse=True)[0]

    def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 10) -> list[SkillManifest]:
        """Semantic search across skill descriptions."""
        # In production, use embedding similarity.
        # Simplified: keyword match on description + tags
        query_lower = query.lower()
        scored = []
        for name, versions in self.skills.items():
            latest = self.get_latest(name)
            if not latest:
                continue
            score = 0
            for keyword in query_lower.split():
                if keyword in latest.description.lower():
                    score += 2
                if keyword in str(latest.tags).lower():
                    score += 3
                if keyword in latest.name.lower():
                    score += 1
            if score > 0:
                scored.append((score, latest))

        scored.sort(key=lambda x: -x[0])
        return [s[1] for s in scored[:top_k]]

    def search_by_capability(self, input_schema: dict, output_schema: dict) -> list[SkillManifest]:
        """Find skills matching a given input/output contract."""
        matches = []
        for name, versions in self.skills.items():
            latest = self.get_latest(name)
            if not latest:
                continue
            # Check schema compatibility
            if (self._schema_matches(latest.input_schema, input_schema) and
                self._schema_matches(latest.output_schema, output_schema)):
                matches.append(latest)
        return matches

    def _schema_matches(self, a: dict, b: dict) -> bool:
        """Check if schema 'a' can satisfy schema 'b'."""
        # Simplified: check that all required B keys exist in A
        b_required = set(b.get("required", []))
        a_properties = set(a.get("properties", {}).keys())
        return b_required.issubset(a_properties)

    def _index(self, manifest: SkillManifest):
        """Index skill for vector search."""
        # In production: use text-embedding-3-small to embed description + tags
        pass

    def deprecate(self, name: str, migration_target: str = None):
        """Mark a skill as deprecated."""
        for version in self.skills.get(name, []):
            version.status = "deprecated"

    def get_team_skills(self, team: str) -> list[SkillManifest]:
        """Get all skills owned by a team."""
        return [
            self.get_latest(name)
            for name, versions in self.skills.items()
            if any(v.owner_team == team for v in versions)
        ]


# Staff insight:
# The registry should NOT require central approval for skill registration.
# Use a pull model: anyone can register, consumers discover and choose.
# Quality emerges from metrics (usage, success rate) not gatekeeping.

sections.title: Semantic Kernel Skills Pattern
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Microsoft's Semantic Kernel (SK) is the most mature open-source skill framework. It defines three skill types. Native skills: Python functions decorated with @sk_function that perform deterministic operations (API calls, calculations, DB queries). Semantic skills: prompt templates with configuration (temperature, max tokens, stop sequences) that call an LLM. OpenAPI skills: auto-generated from OpenAPI specs — any REST API becomes a skill. The SK kernel orchestrates skill execution: it resolves dependencies, manages context, and handles prompt rendering. The key insight: SK treats prompts as first-class code — prompt templates are versioned, tested, and deployed like any other code artifact.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Semantic Kernel is used by 1000+ organizations (Microsoft 2026). A typical SK deployment: 20-100 skills, 200-1000 functions, 50-500 prompt templates. The planner (which selects and sequences skills) achieves 80-90% plan quality for 3-5 skill chains. SK's Java SDK is used in Android AI features. SK's Python SDK is used in Microsoft Copilot for M365. Performance: kernel overhead is <1ms per skill call (cached plans).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Install Semantic Kernel (pip install semantic-kernel). 2) Create a native skill: write a Python class with @sk_function decorated methods. 3) Create a semantic skill: write a skprompt.txt with the prompt template and config.json with LLM settings. 4) Register skills with the kernel. 5) Use the SK planner to automatically compose skills: describe the goal and the planner sequences the right skills. 6) Deploy the kernel as a service behind your agent runtime.
sections.codeBlock:
# Semantic Kernel: Native + Semantic Skills
import semantic_kernel as sk
from semantic_kernel.skill_definition import sk_function, sk_function_context_parameter
from semantic_kernel.orchestration.sk_context import SKContext

# === Native Skill (Python code) ===
class DatabaseSkill:
    """
    Native skill: executes Python code, calls databases.
    Deterministic, fast, no LLM call.
    """
    @sk_function(
        description="Execute a SQL query and return results",
        name="query_database"
    )
    @sk_function_context_parameter(
        name="query",
        description="SQL query to execute (SELECT only)"
    )
    @sk_function_context_parameter(
        name="connection_string",
        description="Database connection string"
    )
    async def query_database(self, context: SKContext) -> str:
        query = context["query"]
        conn_str = context["connection_string"]
        # In production: use connection pool, parameterized queries
        import sqlite3
        conn = sqlite3.connect(conn_str)
        cursor = conn.execute(query)
        results = cursor.fetchall()
        conn.close()
        return str(results)


# === Semantic Skill (prompt template) ===
# File: skills/SummarizeSkill/skprompt.txt
# """
# Summarize the following text in under 100 words:
# 
# {{$input}}
# 
# Summary:
# """
#
# File: skills/SummarizeSkill/config.json
# {
#   "schema": 1,
#   "description": "Summarizes text concisely",
#   "type": "completion",
#   "completion": {
#     "max_tokens": 200,
#     "temperature": 0.3,
#     "top_p": 0.5
#   }
# }


# === Kernel: orchestrating skills ===
class SemanticKernelRuntime:
    """
    Wraps SK kernel for production use.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.kernel = sk.Kernel()
        self.kernel.add_chat_service("gpt4", sk.connectors.ai.open_ai.OpenAIChatCompletion(
            "gpt-4", "", ""  # api_key, org_id
        ))
        self._register_skills()

    def _register_skills(self):
        # Register native skills
        db_skill = self.kernel.import_skill(DatabaseSkill(), "database")

        # Register semantic skills from filesystem
        self.kernel.import_semantic_skill_from_directory(
            "./skills", "summarize"
        )

    def run_skill(self, skill_name: str, function_name: str, input: str) -> str:
        """Execute a specific skill function."""
        result = self.kernel.run_async(
            self.kernel.skills[skill_name][function_name],
            input_str=input
        )
        return result.result

    def auto_compose(self, goal: str) -> list[str]:
        """
        Use SK's planner to automatically compose skills for a goal.
        The planner generates a plan: sequence of skills + parameters.
        """
        from semantic_kernel.planning.basic_planner import BasicPlanner
        planner = BasicPlanner()
        plan = planner.create_plan_async(self.kernel, goal)
        # Execute the plan
        result = plan.invoke_async(self.kernel)
        return [step.description for step in plan._steps]


# Staff Insight:
# SK's key innovation: prompts as code (versioned, tested, deployed).
# Treat your prompt templates with the same rigor as your Python code.
# Test prompt changes with A/B evaluation before deploying.

sections.title: Skill Security & Governance
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Skills are the unit of governance in an AI system. Each skill has a defined capability scope (what it can do), data access (what data it sees), and execution context (what permissions it runs with). Governing skills is easier than governing agents because skills are bounded and predictable. The governance model: each skill declares its required permissions at registration, the registry enforces that skills only access permitted resources, and agents inherit the union of their composed skills' permissions. This principle-of-least-privilege applies at the skill level.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Skill-level governance reduces the blast radius of a compromised agent by 10-100x. If an agent uses 5 skills, a compromise only gives access to those 5 skills' capabilities, not the entire system. Permission declaration overhead: 2-5 lines per skill. Audit log volume: 10-50x less than agent-level logging (skills log at a coarser granularity).

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Define permission scopes (read:users, write:tickets, execute:search, admin:all). 2) Each skill declares its required scopes in its manifest. 3) The registry validates that declared scopes match actual code (via static analysis). 4) At runtime, skill execution runs in a sandbox that enforces the declared permissions. 5) Audit logs record which skill was called, by which agent, with what permissions.
sections.codeBlock:
# Skill governance: permissions and sandboxing
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any
import os, subprocess, json

@dataclass
class SkillPermissions:
    """Declared permissions for a skill."""
    data_access: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # e.g., ["read:customers", "read:tickets"]
    write_access: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)  # e.g., ["write:tickets"]
    network_access: bool = False
    code_execution: bool = False
    max_execution_time_s: float = 30.0
    max_memory_mb: int = 500


class SkillSandbox:
    """
    Executes skills in a sandboxed environment.
    Enforces declared permissions at runtime.
    """
    def __init__(self, permissions: SkillPermissions):
        self.perms = permissions

    async def execute(self, skill_fn: callable, **kwargs) -> Any:
        """Execute skill with sandbox enforcement."""
        # In production, this would run in a subprocess/container:
        # subprocess.run(["sandbox", "--allow-read=/data/customers",
        #                 "--timeout=30", "--memory=500MB",
        #                 "python", "-c", skill_code])

        # For this example, we check permissions in-process
        self._check_execution_permissions()

        import signal

        class TimeoutError(Exception):
            pass

        def handler(signum, frame):
            raise TimeoutError(f"Skill exceeded {self.perms.max_execution_time_s}s")

        signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, handler)
        signal.alarm(int(self.perms.max_execution_time_s))

        try:
            result = await skill_fn(**kwargs)
            return result
        except TimeoutError as e:
            return {"error": str(e)}
        finally:
            signal.alarm(0)

    def _check_execution_permissions(self):
        if self.perms.code_execution:
            # Only allow if explicitly declared
            pass
        if not self.perms.network_access:
            # Block network calls
            # In subprocess: prevent via seccomp/AppArmor
            pass


# === Governance Audit Trail ===
class SkillAuditLogger:
    def __init__(self):
        self.logs: list[dict] = []

    def log_call(self, skill_name: str, agent_id: str, user_id: str,
                 permissions: SkillPermissions, success: bool, duration_ms: float):
        entry = {
            "timestamp": __import__('time').time(),
            "skill_name": skill_name,
            "agent_id": agent_id,
            "user_id": user_id,
            "permissions": permissions.__dict__,
            "success": success,
            "duration_ms": duration_ms
        }
        self.logs.append(entry)
        # In production: write to secure audit store (immutable log)

    def get_skill_usage(self, skill_name: str) -> dict:
        calls = [l for l in self.logs if l["skill_name"] == skill_name]
        success_rate = sum(1 for c in calls if c["success"]) / max(len(calls), 1)
        return {
            "total_calls": len(calls),
            "success_rate": round(success_rate, 3),
            "avg_duration_ms": sum(c["duration_ms"] for c in calls) / max(len(calls), 1)
        }

sections.title: FAANG Q&A — AI Skills Architecture
sections.description:
search Q1 (Microsoft L7): "Design a skill sharing system for a company with 500 developers building AI features." A: Three layers. (1) Registry: centralized catalog with semantic search, versioning, and quality gates. (2) SDK: a Python package for building skills with decorators, auto-manifest generation, and local testing. (3) Marketplace: a UI for browsing, testing, and subscribing to skills. Key challenges: preventing dependency hell (skill A depends on skill B v1, skill C depends on B v2), handling skill deprecation (graceful migration windows), and ensuring backward compatibility (contract testing).

search Q2 (Google L6): "How do you decide if a capability should be a tool, a skill, or an agent?" A: Three questions. (1) Does it need state or multi-step logic? If no, it's a tool. (2) Does it need dynamic planning? If yes, it's an agent. Otherwise, it's a skill. (3) Will it be reused by multiple teams? If yes, make it a skill (the unit of reuse). In practice, start as a tool, evolve to a skill when you add state/composition, graduate to an agent when you add planning. Most capabilities should stay as skills.

search Q3 (Anthropic L5): "How do you test a skill that depends on an LLM?" A: Three testing strategies. (1) Deterministic testing: mock the LLM call, test the skill logic with known inputs/outputs. (2) Scenario testing: use a fixed LLM response set for common scenarios, test skill behavior. (3) Eval testing: run the skill against a golden dataset with real LLM calls, measure quality metrics. In CI/CD, run (1) and (2) on every commit, run (3) nightly. A skill that passes all three tests is production-ready.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Semantic Kernel Documentation — Microsoft's AI orchestration SDK. The reference implementation for skill-based AI architecture.
  • Semantic Kernel GitHub — Open-source SDK for Python, C#, Java. Skills, planners, memory, and connectors.
  • Compositional Skills for Language Agents — Academic study on skill composition for LLM agents. Proposes hierarchical skill decomposition.
  • Skill Induction and Planning with Language Models — Research on automatically inducing reusable skills from demonstrations.

details:
Skills Architecture Decision Flow: Start by identifying business capabilities that can be encapsulated as skills (search, summarize, classify, extract, transform). Each skill has single responsibility and a clear contract. Register skills in a central registry with versioning and search. Use Semantic Kernel for the runtime if you need mature skill orchestration. Compose skills via chaining, routing, parallel fan-out, and fallback chains. Add governance at the skill level (permissions, sandboxing, audit). Skills are the unit of reuse, sharing, and governance — they're the most important abstraction in production AI systems.

Related: Tool Use & Function Calling | MCP — Model Context Protocol | Advanced Agent Architectures
